articles.title: A System in Motion: The Evolution of Orthopaedic Care in Romania.
articles.authors: Dragosloveanu S, Nedelea DG, Scheau C
articles.population: Orthopaedic patients in Romania
articles.outcomes: Digital infrastructure adoption, robotic-assisted surgery use, artificial intelligence applications, hospital bed capacity, arthroplasty register modernisation
articles.take_home: Romania has invested in modern orthopaedic care with robotic surgery and artificial intelligence, has high hospital bed capacity to support elderly and complex patients, and has recently updated its joint replacement register to match other European countries.

articles.title: Salvage total hip arthroplasty after internal fixation compared with acute total hip arthroplasty for fracture: a cohort study based on 32,960 cases from the Dutch Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Van Marle L, Peters RM, Van Steenbergen LN, Jaarsma RL, Doornberg JN, Zijlstra WP
articles.study_type: Cohort study using national arthroplasty register
articles.population: Patients who had hip replacement surgery for hip fractures, either as initial treatment or after failed internal fixation
articles.sample_size: 32,960 procedures (12,486 salvage hip replacements after failed fixation; 18,450 acute hip replacements for fracture)
articles.outcomes: Revision hip replacement rates at 1 and 5 years; reasons for revision; factors associated with increased revision risk
articles.take_home: Hip replacements performed to rescue a failed internal fixation had similar revision rates to hip replacements done immediately after fracture. Previous internal fixation does not appear to worsen the outcome of hip replacement surgery.

articles.title: Treatment and outcomes of fractures in children: a registry-based cohort study of 10,144 fractures.
articles.authors: Laaksonen T, Grahn P, Ahonen M, Ahola JA, Helenius I, Nietosvaara Y
articles.population: Children with fractures treated at a Level I trauma centre in Helsinki
articles.outcomes: Surgical timing, treatment methods, complication rates, unplanned reoperations, permanent nerve injuries, deep infections
articles.take_home: Most common childhood fractures were treated safely with very low rates of serious complications like nerve damage (0.04%) and infections (0.3%). Many forearm fractures were successfully managed without general anaesthesia in the emergency department.

articles.title: Reliability and clinical utility of paediatric ankle fracture classifications : a systematic review.
articles.authors: Teo LS, Xiao L, Yeo BKK, Rayapati R, Lim AKS, Hoipo Hui J, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review of classification system reliability studies
articles.population: Paediatric ankle fractures
articles.outcomes: Inter- and intraobserver reliability of classification systems; clinical utility in guiding treatment decisions and predicting outcomes including premature physeal closure and angular deformity
articles.take_home: The Dias-Tachdjian classification system was most reliable for doctors to use consistently when classifying children's ankle fractures, whilst the Salter-Harris system was most commonly used and best at predicting whether growth plate damage would occur. Using both classification systems together may help doctors plan treatment more accurately.

articles.title: Diagnostic value of serum troponin in stable patients at risk for blunt cardiac injury.
articles.authors: Cleary HL, Bernard MD, Davenport DL, Bernard AC
articles.population: Adult trauma patients with blunt cardiac injury or sternal fracture who were haemodynamically stable
articles.outcomes: Adverse cardiac events (new arrhythmia requiring treatment, cardiac surgery, or cardiac-related mortality)
articles.take_home: In stable patients admitted after blunt chest trauma, measuring troponin levels did not help predict whether they would develop heart problems. The study suggests that stable patients may not need troponin testing and can be safely monitored without it.

articles.title: The fate of stage I Masquelet cement spacers in long bone reconstruction for acute fractures.
articles.authors: Dekle JK, Sisk CK, Litten RM, Alcaide DM, McIlwain RN, Wilson AL, et al.
articles.population: Adult patients undergoing Masquelet reconstruction for acute long bone fractures
articles.sample_size: 156 patients (48 Stage I only, 108 completed both stages)
articles.outcomes: Stage II completion rates, fracture-related infection, hardware removal, wound dehiscence, amputation, mortality
articles.take_home: About one-third of patients who had the first stage of Masquelet reconstruction did not go on to have the second stage, most commonly because of amputation. However, among patients followed for at least 6 months, the overall rates of complications and amputation were similar between those who had one stage and those who completed both stages.

articles.title: Efficacy and limitations of a web-based native adult knee septic arthritis calculator in urban trauma care.
articles.authors: Serbin RP, Burger J, Odum S, Chandler C, Welch S, Di Napoli N, et al.
articles.population: Adult patients with suspected knee septic arthritis undergoing arthrocentesis at a tertiary referral centre
articles.outcomes: Diagnostic accuracy of web-based calculator; optimal risk thresholds for surgical intervention and excluding septic arthritis
articles.take_home: A web-based calculator using six clinical and laboratory measures showed excellent accuracy at distinguishing septic from non-septic knee infections. A calculated probability of 58% or higher suggested the need for surgery, whilst below 24.6% made it safe to rule out infection.

articles.title: External validation of machine learning models for estimation of mortality 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture on 5,055 consecutive patients.
articles.authors: Mosfeldt M, Jorgensen HL, Lauritzen JB, Jansson KA
articles.study_type: External validation of machine learning models
articles.population: Patients with hip fractures treated at 2 hospitals in Stockholm, Sweden
articles.outcomes: Mortality prediction at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture using machine learning models (Random Forest, eXtreme Gradient Boosting, and Generalized Linear Models)
articles.take_home: Machine learning models, particularly eXtreme Gradient Boosting, can reliably predict how many patients with hip fractures will survive at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after their injury. The models worked well when tested on new patients and have been made available online to help clinicians discuss survival chances with individual patients.

articles.title: CT-based micromotion analysis for tracking fracture displacement and fragment movement in trochanteric hip fractures: an agreement study.
articles.authors: Aberg H, Hanni M, Sandberg O, Jonsson KB
articles.study_type: Prospective cohort study with measurement agreement analysis
articles.population: Patients with trochanteric hip fractures treated with cephalomedullary nail
articles.outcomes: Measurement repeatability of CT-based micromotion analysis for fracture displacement, surgical reduction accuracy, and postoperative fragment movement
articles.take_home: CT-based micromotion analysis can precisely measure hip fracture displacement and movement in three dimensions with excellent repeatability. After surgery, fractures remained stable during healing with small amounts of expected movement including femoral shortening and inward shift.

articles.title: Fear of reinjury is associated with failure to return to preinjury level of activity after operative repair of ankle fractures.
articles.authors: Hadley SM Jr, Peabody JJ, Durudogan EH, Westvold SJ, Bergman R, Chang S, et al.
articles.population: Patients who underwent operative repair of ankle fractures at a single academic medical centre between 2016 and 2021
articles.outcomes: Association between fear of reinjury and failure to return to preinjury activity level
articles.take_home: All patients who experienced fear of reinjury failed to return to their preinjury activity level after ankle fracture surgery, compared with only 12% of patients without fear. Fear of reinjury may be a key barrier to recovery and was more common in people with higher activity levels before injury.

articles.title: What is the desired screw trajectory for fixation of noncontiguous posterior distal articular fractures associated with distal tibial shaft fractures?
articles.authors: Moon TJ, Blumenschein LA, Swetz AM, Nedder VJ, Ochenjele G, Wetzel RJ, et al.
articles.population: Patients with distal tibial shaft fractures associated with nondisplaced posterior distal articular fractures
articles.sample_size: 56 posterior distal articular fractures in 74 patients
articles.outcomes: Optimal screw trajectory angles and starting points for perpendicular fixation of posterior distal articular fractures
articles.take_home: A standard screw angle and starting point would only work correctly in about half of cases in one direction and three-quarters in another direction. Computed tomography scans should be used before surgery to plan the best screw position for each individual patient rather than using a one-size-fits-all approach.

articles.title: CTA-detected incidence and descriptive fracture patterns of arterial injury in 539 surgically treated tibial plateau fractures.
articles.authors: Valderrama J, Narvaez F, Cancino J, Raffo JF, Bosselin R, Zamora F, et al.
articles.population: Patients with surgically treated tibial plateau fractures at a level I trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Incidence of arterial injury detected on CTA; fracture patterns associated with arterial injury
articles.take_home: Arterial injuries were found in just over 1% of surgically treated tibial plateau fractures. The study identified some fracture patterns and clinical scenarios that appeared more common when arterial injury was present, but these observations were based on very few cases and need further investigation.

articles.title: Risk Factors for Failure of Repair of Diaphyseal Tibial Nonunions: A Retrospective Cohort Study of 108 Patients.
articles.authors: Fan S, Wagner RK, Lehle CH, Musick AN, Gregg AT, Janssen SJ, et al.
articles.population: Adults aged 18 years and over with non-healing breaks in the middle section of the shin bone treated with surgical repair
articles.outcomes: Rate of additional surgery needed for continued non-healing; radiographic evidence of bone healing at final follow-up
articles.take_home: About 1 in 4 patients needed further surgery for persistent non-healing, and 1 in 25 eventually required amputation. Smoking, initial open fractures and bone defects increased the risk of needing more surgery, but 9 in 10 patients eventually achieved radiographic healing.

articles.title: Rethinking the Lisfranc injury: Rationale for and development of a new classification system for Lisfranc injuries.
articles.authors: Cawley WCF, Hwang P, Mittal R, Zochowski Y, Darwish I, O'Keefe R, et al.
articles.study_type: Development and validation of a new classification system using a retrospective case series
articles.population: Patients with indirect Lisfranc injuries (injuries to joints in the midfoot)
articles.outcomes: Inter-observer and intra-observer reliability of the new Suthersan classification system compared to the existing Myerson-Hardcastle classification
articles.take_home: The new Suthersan classification system for Lisfranc injuries showed much better agreement between doctors (0.95 compared to 0.65) and was more consistent when the same doctor reviewed cases again (97.7% compared to 87.9%), suggesting it could be a more reliable tool for identifying these midfoot injuries.

articles.title: Injury and trauma care training in global health: A scoping review of costs and cost-effectiveness across resource settings.
articles.authors: Phan HT, Nguyen BT, Zhao T, Phung LT, Keakabetse TR, Dinh TTN, et al.
articles.study_type: Scoping review of costs and cost-effectiveness
articles.population: Injury and trauma care training programmes across low-, middle- and high-income countries
articles.outcomes: Implementation costs (ranging from USD 3.70 to USD 1433.5 per participant), cost-effectiveness of training programmes, impact of training-of-trainers and cascading models on costs
articles.take_home: Most trauma care training programmes showed good value for money, and using trained instructors to teach others (rather than foreign experts) can cut costs by up to 28% without reducing training quality. However, more consistent research methods are needed, especially in regions with high injury rates.

articles.title: Mechanism of injury not associated with acute compartment syndrome in Tibia fractures.
articles.authors: Egbe S, Marks D, Christiano AV, Erdman MK, Wolf JM, Strelzow J
articles.population: Skeletally mature patients with tibial fractures
articles.outcomes: Incidence of acute compartment syndrome (ACS) and associations with injury mechanism, fracture location, morphology, and patient characteristics
articles.take_home: Acute compartment syndrome occurred in only 2.1% of tibial fractures overall, and the type of injury (blunt versus ballistic) did not increase the risk. However, vascular injury was significantly associated with developing compartment syndrome, so doctors need to remain vigilant for this serious complication regardless of how the injury occurred.

articles.title: Psychological outlook and recovery after orthopaedic trauma: Evidence and implications for Central India and other low-resource settings.
articles.authors: Salphale YS, Saodekar H, Kimmatkar N, Shinde G, Kothari P, Salphale AY
articles.study_type: Narrative review of observational and interventional studies
articles.population: Adult patients with orthopaedic trauma, with emphasis on low- and middle-income countries including India
articles.outcomes: Pain, disability, functional recovery, quality of life, rehabilitation adherence, and psychological distress (depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress)
articles.take_home: About one-quarter to one-half of orthopaedic trauma patients experience significant psychological distress that worsens pain and disability independent of injury severity. Negative thinking patterns like catastrophising delay recovery, whilst positive outlooks like optimism and social support improve rehabilitation outcomes, suggesting that screening for psychological problems and offering culturally appropriate support could help improve recovery in low-resource settings.

articles.title: Peterson vs. Salter Harris physeal fracture classification systems: How consistent are they?
articles.authors: Charles-Lozoya S, Cobos-Aguilar H, Valdez-Cordova C, de la Parra-Marquez ML, Manilla-Munoz E, Arriaga-Cazares HE, et al.
articles.population: Paediatric orthopaedic surgeons and orthopaedic residents
articles.sample_size: 100 radiographs classified by 10 observers (5 experts, 5 residents)
articles.outcomes: Intraobserver and interobserver agreement for Salter-Harris versus Peterson physeal fracture classification systems, measured by kappa scores and percentage agreement
articles.take_home: Both the Salter-Harris and Peterson classification systems show similar reliability for classifying growth plate fractures in children, with no meaningful difference between them. This suggests either system can be used clinically, though agreement between different observers remains only moderate.

articles.title: Do reduced screws have a significant impact on fixation in medial open wedge high tibial osteotomy? A comparative fatigue and stability analysis.
articles.authors: Ozkaya M
articles.population: Not applicable - biomechanical study
articles.outcomes: Stiffness, deformation, tibial plateau displacement, stress levels, and fatigue life under physiological loading
articles.take_home: Using two screws instead of three in high tibial osteotomy plates provides similar stability and strength in most situations, though the two-screw design may not last as long under certain backwards-directed forces. Two screws could be a useful option as it uses less bone, reduces surgery time and may lower infection risk.

articles.title: Moving beyond mortality measurements after road traffic injuries: Ten-year death risk after road traffic injuries.
articles.authors: Cherchiglia ML, Mougin V, Gray A, Wool EE, Malta DC, Melo APS, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using record-linkage methodology
articles.population: People aged 15 and over hospitalised for road traffic injuries in Brazil between 2000 and 2015, compared with those hospitalised for other reasons
articles.outcomes: All-cause and cause-specific mortality risk over three months to ten years after hospitalisation
articles.take_home: People hospitalised for road traffic injuries had more than three times the risk of death in the following years compared with those hospitalised for other reasons, with particularly high risks in young and middle-aged men and increased deaths from substance use, violence and suicide.

articles.title: Overutilization of helicopter emergency medical services compared to ground transport in moderate-severe penetrating abdominal trauma & associated outcomes.
articles.authors: Hus A, Brown A, Bundschu I, Prashar S, Kumar S, Indorewala Y, et al.
articles.population: Adult trauma patients aged 18 years or older with moderate-to-severe penetrating abdominal injuries in hemorrhagic shock requiring massive transfusion protocol
articles.outcomes: Primary: 24-hour and in-hospital mortality. Secondary: transfusion requirements, intervention rate, and complications
articles.take_home: Ground ambulances and helicopters had similar outcomes for patients with serious abdominal stab or gunshot wounds when arrival time at hospital was less than 10 minutes. Helicopters may be overused in some cases and resources should be allocated more carefully based on transport time.

articles.title: Early postoperative anxiety is associated with patient-reported recovery after hip fracture: A PROMIS-based longitudinal cohort study.
articles.authors: Achebe C, Cureton R, Rothberg D, Higgins T, Marchand L, Haller J
articles.population: Adults aged over 55 years with hip fracture treated with surgery
articles.sample_size: 304 patients for physical function analysis, 285 patients for pain interference analysis
articles.outcomes: Achievement of clinically meaningful improvement in physical function and pain interference within one year after surgery
articles.take_home: Patients who experienced higher anxiety in the first month after hip fracture surgery were less likely to achieve meaningful improvements in physical function and pain over the following year. The study suggests that early anxiety may be an important sign that patients need extra support to recover well.

articles.title: Association between neighborhood opportunity and incidence of pediatric trauma.
articles.authors: Monroy A, Tan JM, Arbogast H, Spurrier R, Nager A, Chaudhari PP
articles.population: Children under 18 years old with home zip codes in Los Angeles County
articles.outcomes: Trauma activations per 100,000 children; injury mechanism, severity and disposition by neighbourhood opportunity score
articles.take_home: Children living in lower opportunity neighbourhoods had higher rates of trauma from being struck as pedestrians or cyclists, firearm injuries and assaults, whilst children in higher opportunity areas had more falls and sports injuries. This suggests injury prevention programmes should be tailored to the specific risks in different neighbourhoods.

articles.title: Satisfaction with nursing care among Jordanian burn patients admitted to burn critical care units: A cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Al-Ghabeesh S, Alnaeem M, Qaddoura N, Ali BA, Al-Masalha HA
articles.population: Adult burn patients admitted to a burn intensive care unit in Jordan
articles.outcomes: Patient satisfaction with nursing care measured using the Arabic Patient Satisfaction with Nursing Care Quality Questionnaire; associations with demographic and clinical factors
articles.take_home: Burn patients in intensive care reported moderate satisfaction with nursing care. Patients with higher income were more satisfied, while those with facial deformity were less satisfied, suggesting that socioeconomic and visible injury factors influence how satisfied patients are with their nursing care.

articles.title: Flexible intramedullary nailing in the treatment of paediatric forearm fractures: 15-years' experience at a level one trauma centre, comparison of implant retention versus removal and cost-effectiveness analysis.
articles.authors: Ismayl G, Alabbas A, Howard A, Fishlock A, Sabouni MY
articles.population: Children with forearm fractures treated with flexible intramedullary nailing
articles.outcomes: Secondary surgery rates, time to nail removal, patient satisfaction, cost-effectiveness
articles.take_home: Leaving the nails in place after healing was safe and did not lead to more unplanned operations compared with routine removal, whilst also saving money. Patient satisfaction remained very high in both groups.

articles.title: Association of Primary Trauma Care training with mortality in isolated traumatic brain injury: A sub-analysis of the PISTACHIO study in Vietnam.
articles.authors: Nguyen BT, Lam TT, Vu NHN, Pham TH, Do TD, Nguyen TTN, et al.
articles.population: Patients with isolated traumatic brain injury at two provincial hospitals in Vietnam
articles.outcomes: 24-hour and 30-day mortality rates
articles.take_home: Primary Trauma Care training was associated with a significant reduction in mortality for patients with isolated head injury, with 24-hour mortality falling from 6.7% to 1.8%. This benefit was maintained at 30 days, suggesting that early stabilisation of breathing and airway may help prevent secondary brain injury.

articles.title: Cemented versus uncemented femoral stem fixation in hip arthroplasty for displaced femoral neck fractures in elderly patients: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Malki AT, Babtain HW, Alharbi DM, Shahhatalsayed AA, Alshaban RM, Almaghrabi YT, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials
articles.population: Elderly patients with displaced femoral neck fractures undergoing hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Mortality, periprosthetic fracture, dislocation, and wound infection
articles.take_home: Cemented femoral stems were associated with lower death rates and significantly lower rates of fracture around the implant compared with uncemented stems, with no increase in dislocation or infection risk. Cemented fixation appears to be the safer choice for elderly patients with femoral neck fractures.

articles.title: Impact of surgical timing on complications and mortality in periprosthetic hip fractures compared to native hip fractures.
articles.authors: Aerden LKP, Brouwers X, Sermon A, Ghijselings S, Vles G, Herteleer M, et al.
articles.population: Patients with periprosthetic hip fractures treated surgically at a university trauma centre, compared with a benchmark cohort of native hip fracture patients
articles.outcomes: Time to surgery, surgical complications, mortality at 30, 60, 90 days and six months, length of stay, overall survival
articles.take_home: Periprosthetic hip fracture patients had longer delays to surgery than native hip fracture patients, mainly due to surgeon availability and logistics rather than patient factors. Despite these delays, complications and overall survival were not significantly worse, suggesting that having the right surgical expertise may be more important than speed for these fractures.

articles.title: Antibiotic prophylaxis for preventing open fracture related infections - A scoping review.
articles.authors: Krakau RB, Hamilton ND, Dastrup AS, Klein K, Dreyer CH
articles.study_type: Scoping review of antibiotic prophylaxis literature
articles.population: Patients with open fractures in Denmark, Finland, Germany, Great Britain, Iceland, the Netherlands, Norway, and Sweden
articles.outcomes: Antibiotic prophylaxis recommendations for different grades of open fracture (Gustilo-Anderson classification) to prevent fracture-related infections
articles.take_home: Most studies recommend cephalosporins for less severe open fractures, but suggest additional antibiotics covering gram-negative bacteria for more severe fractures. Danish regional guidelines currently differ on when to use broader antibiotic coverage, and better-quality research is needed to establish optimal treatment strategies.

articles.title: Prevalence and presenting symptoms of intracranial hemorrhage in older adults presenting with subacute head injuries in a level-2 trauma center: A retrospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Raats JH, Schuijt HJ, Hoogervorst ELJ, Velde DV
articles.population: Adults aged over 65 years presenting to emergency department with subacute head injuries (more than 48 hours after initial trauma)
articles.outcomes: Traumatic intracranial haemorrhage prevalence, hospital length of stay, anticoagulation reversal, neurosurgical intervention, 90-day mortality, and symptoms associated with intracranial haemorrhage
articles.take_home: About 1 in 6 older adults presenting with subacute head injuries had intracranial bleeding, with most cases being serious. Symptoms like headache, vomiting, focal weakness and gait problems were more common in those with bleeding, though overall outcomes were reasonably good with appropriate treatment.

articles.title: The use of external fixation in the treatment of nonunion and malunion of the upper extremity.
articles.authors: Al Ashhab MGED, Abdelnaser A, Elashhab AM, Elmesalamy N, Hosny G
articles.population: Patients with nonunion or malunion of humeral and forearm fractures treated with external fixation
articles.sample_size: 45 patients (7 malunions, 8 aseptic nonunions, 30 septic nonunions)
articles.outcomes: Complete radiological union in all cases, mechanical alignment restored in most patients, mean pain score 2.1/10, mean patient satisfaction 8.7/10, superficial pin-tract infections managed with local care and antibiotics
articles.take_home: External fixation using Ilizarov principles successfully healed all nonunions and malunions of upper arm bones, even complex cases with infection or previous failed operations. Patients had good pain control and satisfaction, though the treatment took on average 7.9 months.

articles.title: Analysing geospatial variables in the context of trauma networks: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Harris M, Mosali T, Doyle F, Watts L
articles.population: Trauma patients in trauma networks across seventeen countries
articles.outcomes: Mortality, injury severity, prehospital time, transport distance, spatial access to care, and impact of helicopter emergency medical services
articles.take_home: The distance patients live from trauma centres and how long it takes to reach care significantly affect survival, with rural patients having worse outcomes due to longer travel times and limited services. Helicopter ambulances improved access and survival in some areas, but how trauma networks measure and report these factors needs to be standardised across regions.

articles.title: The use of temporary intravascular shunting to improve resource utilization is safe and effective for penetrating peripheral vascular trauma.
articles.authors: Strobl HV, Coopwood CK, Bolaji T, McKinley WI, Hoefer LE, Polcari AM, et al.
articles.population: Patients with penetrating peripheral arterial injuries treated at a Level 1 trauma centre
articles.sample_size: 228 patients (118 immediate repair, 47 delayed repair for resource utilisation, 63 delayed repair for damage control)
articles.outcomes: Vascular complications requiring reoperation, compartment syndrome, amputation, and mortality
articles.take_home: Temporary intravascular shunting to free up operating room resources is safe and effective for penetrating peripheral vascular injuries, with no significant difference in complications or death compared to immediate repair. Shunts remained in place for a median of about 13 hours before definitive repair.

articles.title: Validation of a novel osteoarticular fracture fragment decontamination-preservation system for delayed articular fracture reconstruction for limb preservation in a preclinical canine model pilot study.
articles.authors: Cook JL, Della Rocca GJ, Bozynski CC, Stoker AM, Cook CR, Crist BD, et al.
articles.population: Dogs with induced open bicondylar femoral fractures
articles.sample_size: 6 dogs (3 with bacterial contamination, 3 with bacterial and MRSA contamination)
articles.outcomes: Complete eradication of pathogens, maintenance of viable cartilage cell density, fracture healing, restoration of limb function and knee range of motion to over 80% of pre-operative levels
articles.take_home: A novel decontamination and preservation system successfully removed dangerous bacteria (including antibiotic-resistant MRSA) from contaminated bone and cartilage fragments whilst keeping cartilage cells viable, allowing these fragments to be used for joint reconstruction and restoration of limb function in dogs. This preclinical study provides early evidence that the technique might help preserve severely damaged joints in open fractures.

articles.title: Superior survival, hemodynamics, and metabolic recovery using PEG-20k-based low volume resuscitation in a polytrauma hemorrhagic shock model in rats.
articles.authors: Khoraki J, Payne C, Lust D, Mangino MJ, Liebrecht LK
articles.population: Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats
articles.outcomes: Survival time, blood pressure (MAP), blood lactate levels, and blood loss
articles.take_home: A new intravenous fluid called PEG-20k significantly improved survival and blood pressure recovery in rats with severe trauma and severe bleeding, compared to standard fluids. This shelf-stable fluid could potentially help patients in remote locations or situations where blood products are not available.

articles.title: The forequarter lateral implosion injury: A diagnostic paradigm and Ch-Sh (chvarepsilon-sha) classification.
articles.authors: George A, Paull TZ, Winter JD, Levine J, Cole PA
articles.population: Patients with combined chest wall and shoulder girdle injuries from lateral impact trauma
articles.outcomes: ICU admission, pneumothorax, pulmonary contusion, mechanical ventilation, spinal or other orthopedic fractures, Injury Severity Score, 30-day mortality, and classification system reliability
articles.take_home: A new classification system for combined chest and shoulder injuries from lateral impact helps predict which patients will have worse outcomes. Patients with the most severe chest wall injuries had higher rates of serious complications and death, but unexpectedly, those with multiple shoulder injuries together (rather than isolated scapula or humerus fractures) had better outcomes, possibly because the injuries absorb and spread the force of impact away from vital organs.

articles.title: More than meets the eye: the continuing importance of tertiary trauma survey even in moderate (ISS 9-15) trauma.
articles.authors: Knight C, Burke JR, Hind S, Smith SR
articles.population: Major trauma patients aged over 16 years admitted to a UK Major Trauma Centre
articles.outcomes: Incidence of unrecognised injuries identified at tertiary trauma survey; sensitivity and specificity of the survey; risk factors for unrecognised injuries
articles.take_home: Nearly one in five major trauma patients had injuries that were not spotted in initial assessments but were found during a more thorough examination later. This thorough examination was very good at finding these missed injuries, particularly in the head, face and eye areas.

articles.title: Minimal Movement Restrictions Do Not Increase Hip Dislocations Following Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Before-and-After Study of 10,357 Patients.
articles.authors: Klaassen AD, Willigenburg NW, Musters JWQ, Jager J, Poolman RW
articles.population: Patients who underwent total hip arthroplasty between January 2015 and March 2020
articles.sample_size: 10,357 patients (7,666 with strict movement restrictions, 2,691 with minimal movement restrictions)
articles.outcomes: Hip dislocation within 90 days after surgery. Strict restriction group: 1.46% dislocation rate. Minimal restriction group: 1.93% dislocation rate. No significant difference between groups.
articles.take_home: Patients who followed a minimal movement restriction protocol after hip replacement had similar rates of hip dislocation compared to those with strict restrictions. This suggests that reducing movement restrictions after hip replacement may be safe and could help patients return to normal activities more quickly.
